title: Lesson 422
transcript: Okay, let's talk about dictionary attacks and then rainbow tables and then how we defeat rainbow tables with salts and peppers and so forth. But let's start here. Let's start with dictionary attacks. The whole idea of a dictionary attack is you want to try and find someone's password as efficiently as possible. The simplest, not the most efficient, but the simplest way of finding someone's password is just a simple brute force attack. You just try letter A and then B and then C and D, et cetera, et cetera. You just keep going through the entire character set until you find someone's password. But as long as they have a sufficiently long password, that's gonna take a very long time. Now what attackers realized long ago is that if you know, for instance, that someone speaks English, the probability of their password being an English word is super high. And so this is where the term dictionary attack came from. What attackers did many, many years ago is they would figure out, okay, what language does someone speak? And if they found out that they spoke Spanish, they would take a Spanish dictionary and then they would just iterate through all the words in the Spanish dictionary. And you were much more likely to find the user's password very quickly there. And of course, attackers did the usual substitutions. Like, I don't know about you, but to make my password more secure, I replace all the E's with like threes. I replace the O's with zeros, you know, leet speak, right? We all do this. We probably see eyes with like ones or something or whatever. We all do this to make our passwords more secure, right? Attackers are aware of this. So you do that in a dictionary attack as well. So that's where the name came from. The attackers used to literally use dictionaries to try and figure out someone's password. Not anymore though. What a dictionary attack looks like nowadays is over the last few decades, literally billions with a B, billions of users' credentials have been exposed, billions of usernames and passwords. And so nowadays, what attackers do when they're doing a dictionary attack is they have huge databases, files available to them, of the most common passwords in the world. Because what's happened, in all these billions of credentials that are being exposed, people have compiled lists of the most common passwords in the world. And so if you're an attacker, you get this list of the most common passwords in the world. And then if you want to try and figure out someone's password, just iterate through that list until you find their password. And you have a much higher chance of finding their password by iterating through that list of the most common passwords in the world. So that's what a modern dictionary attack looks like. attackers have compiled these lists of the most common passwords in the world, and then they just iterate through the list, right? Start with 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and then password. And they just go down through the list of the most common passwords in the world. You're much more likely to find someone's password. So that's what a modern dictionary attack looks like. Okay, now let's get to talking about rainbow tables. So let's take a couple steps back here to explain all this. Imagine this, I am creating a new application and I need my users to be able to log into my application. So I'm gonna have to create a database of usernames and passwords. So let's go ahead and do that. So this will be my user table here. I'm gonna have for each user, I'm gonna have their name in here And I'm also going to store their password. So let's put their password in here too. I'm going to add an entry for myself in here. So my username is Rob. And being the imminent security professional than I am. Imminent? Imminent? I don't even know what the word shit is. I'm clearly not that. Being the security professional that I am, I'm going to use this super secure password. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. So there we go. There's my super secure password that I've stored the database. And of course, you're going to have a bunch of other users in here with a bunch more passwords. First question, is this a good way to store users' passwords in plain text? Of course, the answer is no. You never want to do this. You never want to store your users' passwords in plain text. Because if any attacker ever breaks into this database, they instantly have all of your users' passwords. Or if you have a malicious insider, and they're trolling around through some database they're not supposed to be in. Suddenly, they have every user's password. So you never, ever, ever want to store your user's passwords in plain text. So how should you store users' passwords? Well, there's a couple of things you could do, right? One thing you could do is you could encrypt the user's password. So you could take 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and you could run it through, I don't know, the AES algorithm with 256-bit key, and then just store the ciphertext. That would certainly be more secure, but it's not considered to be the best way of storing users' passwords. And here's why. Encryption is... two way, right? If you're just storing ciphertext here, if the attacker ever got their hands on the encryption key, they can easily decrypt all of your user's passwords. So it's not best practice to store the user's passwords as ciphertext. Instead, we want to use something that's one way, that only works in one direction, hashing. So the secure way of storing users' passwords is to not store the password itself, but the hash of the password. So let's say that we run my super secure password, one, two, three, four, five, six, through the MD5 hashing algorithm. And the hash value of that is, I don't know, XR75. It's obviously not gonna be that, but let's just pretend. So what we've done is we've taken my password, one, two, three, four, five, six, we've run it through the MD5 hashing algorithm, and now we're storing that hash value in the database, my hashed password. And this is way better, right? Because hashing is one way. Even if an attacker breaks into our password database here, all they're gonna have is a bunch of hash values, and they're gonna be like, ah! My dastardly plan has been foiled. I can't figure out the user's passwords because they're all hashed. So this seems brilliant. And this is how a ton of systems store users' credentials as hash values. They're passwords. They store them hashed. A ton of systems do this. Here's the problem. As we just discussed with dictionary attacks, what attackers have their hands on nowadays is giant lists of the most common passwords in the world. So they'll have a giant data set that will list out a bunch of passwords. And as I said, the most common password in the world is 123456. And then the past, you know, this data set is just gonna contain a whole bunch more, you know, depending on how large the dataset is, hundreds of thousands, millions, hundreds of millions, billions more passwords. Okay, so the attackers have these lists of the most common passwords in the world. Here's what they've done. They have taken these most common passwords and they've run them through the most common hashing algorithms, like say SHA-1 and say MD-5 and others. So they've taken 123456 and run it through SHA-1, and maybe that's Q79L. And they've run it through MD5. And maybe that's XR75, et cetera, et cetera. And they'll just keep doing this for all the various passwords. Now, technically what I've drawn here is actually called a hash table, but for purposes of the CISSP exam, you can call this a rainbow table. It's technically not a rainbow table, they're a little more complicated, but for purposes of this high-level management level exam, you can call this a rainbow table. Now you can probably spot why this rainbow table here might be kind of useful for the attacker, right? So they break into a company's password database and they go, ah, drat, the user's passwords are all hashed. Then I say, OK, well, no problem. I've got a giant rainbow table. I'm going to take this hash value, and I'm going to do a lookup in my rainbow table. And by the way, this could be a database, and these could be indexed fields. So you're doing a query, you're doing a search on an index field in a database. to look for this hash value. And aha, within milliseconds, the attacker said, aha, we've got a matching hash value, XR75. And they know right away the user's password, 123456. So this is a major issue. By the way, these rainbow tables are extremely easy to get your hands on. They're on any torrent site, all that sort of stuff. I've got lots of these on my computer system. They're not hard to get your hands on. So any reasonably skilled attacker is going to have a pretty good rainbow table. And if your users are using crappy passwords like 123456, guaranteed that crappy password, the hash value of that crappy password is going to be in a rainbow table. And this is a massive problem because now, near instantaneously, the attacker has the user's password, 123456. So we need to solve for this problem. How do we as the defenders defeat rainbow tables? Because this is a major issue. The way that we do this is with what's called salting. So here's what a salt is. A salt, quite literally, is just a random number, a big random number. That's all a salt is. It's just a big random number. So here's what we're gonna do. For each user in our user database here, we're going to create a big random number for them. So for me, my big random number might be 0 2 m K or something like that. It's going to be a big random number, big random value. And you create a different random value, a different salt value for each user in the database. So that's all salt is. It's just a random number. And it's a unique random number for each user in the database. Now, here's where the salt comes in. When you're storing the user's password in the password database, you're not just taking the path. Like, cause remember what my password was, was 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. And we talked about, right? If you hash 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 with say MD5, it's going to be XR 75, which is going to be in every rainbow table that's ever been created. So how do we solve for this? Even if the user has a garbage password, like one, two, three, four, five, six. Well, what we do is before we store the, before we hash the user's password, we first look up and we say, okay, this is Rob and Rob salt value is zero two M. Okay, so we append this nice, long, random number to the user's password, and then we hash all of this. So we run this through a hashing algorithm. And when we run this through a hashing algorithm, what it might end up being is, I don't know, I'm terrible at randomness. RWAK4, something like that. Whatever it is. But the very important point here is it's a different hash value. It's no longer XR75. And the reason my crappy password no longer hashes to XR75 is because we've appended this really nice, big, long, random value onto the end of my password. And we end up with this new hash value. Now, the very important question is, is this new hash value, RWAK4, is that likely to be in a rainbow table? And the answer is no. Provided that this salt value here is a nice big random value, it essentially means that it's extraordinarily unlikely that... this hash value is going to be a rainbow table. These rainbow tables cannot cover the entire key space of every hash, hashing algorithm, right? They're huge. So rainbow tables are not, you know, cannot, They can't cover every single possible hash value. So as long as you've appended on a nice, big, long, random number, and you're using a good hashing algorithm, not like MB5, but you're using like SHA-512 or 256, there's basically zero chance of this hash value being in a rainbow table. Here's the takeaway. The way that we should be storing users password is we should be storing a salted hashed password because salting defeats rainbow tables. A couple of things I want to point out here. Salts are typically stored in plain text. You don't actually care if the attacker can figure out a user salt value. Why? Well, even if an attacker can figure out a user salt value, what it means is they would have to generate basically a brand new rainbow table for each individual user, right? Because each individual user is going to have their own salt value. So the attacker would have to take the salt value, append it to a password, and regenerate an entire rainbow table. These rainbow tables can take an enormous, like months, maybe even years of computational power to generate. And so if you force an attacker to have to generate an entire new rainbow table for every single user in the database, it completely defeats rainbow tables. The attackers just straight back to brute forcing. So that's why you don't actually care if the attacker gets the salt value because it forces them to generate a brand new rainbow table or just basically it forces them back to brute forcing for each individual user. There's another thing I'll point out here. You may have heard of a pepper. Here's the difference. Salts are more secure because a salt is a unique value for each individual user. Whereas a pepper is more like a system setting. where there'd be a pepper, I don't know, 0007212 or something like that. That might be the pepper. And that pepper will be applied to every single user's password before hashing it. So like a pepper is more like a system setting and it's the same pepper for every user. And that's obviously why peppers are a bit less secure. Salts are more secure because salts are unique to each individual user. There's one more little tiny thing I'll add in here. Not on the exam, but just an interesting bit of trivia. Often, the algorithm that's used for the hashing here is an algorithm called bcrypt. If you've never come across a bcrypt before, it is a hashing algorithm that is intentionally, intentionally super inefficient. Like, orders of magnitude less efficient than SHA-1 or MD5. Why would you intentionally want to use a super intentionally super inefficient hacking algorithm? Well, because if you're just hashing a single user's password and storing that in the database, even if your hashing algorithm is like two or three orders of magnitude less efficient, that's still not that much computational power. It doesn't have a huge impact on authenticating users and stuff like that. It's not going to really slow your system down when you're just authenticating a few users. But now if you're the attacker and you need to generate a rainbow table using bcrypt, which is, and bcrypt by the way is configurable. You can configure essentially how inefficient, how many orders of magnitude less efficient bcrypt is. If it's like, if you've configured it to be like 10 orders of magnitude less efficient, That means it's going to take 10 orders of magnitude more effort to generate your rainbow table. These things already take a ton of computational power to produce. If you've made it 10 orders of magnitude more difficult to generate this rainbow table, you've basically made it impossible for the attacker to do that. That's why you might actually want to use an extremely inefficient hashing algorithm here, like bcrypt. So that's kind of a cool idea. So there you go. That is rainbow tables. And if you wait for me here to erase my eight feet of glass here, I'll just show you a couple of quick slides. It'll summarize all of this. It takes a while to erase all this glass. All right, there we go. Good enough. Okay, so what's a rainbow table? Technically what I showed you is a hash table, right? But it's a pre-computed table of hash values for the most common. You take the most common passwords in the world. By the way, here's what a real rainbow table, real hash table looks like. You take the most common passwords in the world and then you run them through the most common hashing algorithms. And these are the real hash values, by the way, for MD5, for SHA1. These are the real hash values for these passwords. And a rainbow table, of course, would be hundreds of millions of passwords, et cetera. and we have to defeat this. So how do we defeat this? Salting. Salting is just a nice long random value. So let's take the password 123456, right? The most common password in the world. The MD5 of that is F447B2A0, et cetera. This hash value is going to be in every rainbow table that's ever been created. And that's a problem. So if your user is using a garbage password like 123456, it's going to be very easy for an attacker to figure out that password using a rainbow table. So how do we defeat this? Well, we add a really nice, big, long, random number onto the password. Like here, we've added on A8FF250, et cetera. We've added on this salt value. And now we hash. And we end up, of course, with a totally different hash value, BA0B05CF, et cetera. is this hash value likely to be in a rainbow table? No. So that's how hashing defeats rainbow tables.
